title: Physical Therapy Discharge Summary
author: Sarah Thompson, PT, DPT

sections.reasonForNote: Physical therapy discharge summary following 6 weeks of home health PT services post-CHF exacerbation

treatmentSummary.totalSessions: 11 of 12 planned sessions completed (1 cancelled due to patient illness — URI)
interventions: Progressive walking program (advanced from 5 minutes to 15 minutes continuous walking)
Lower extremity strengthening: sit-to-stand, mini-squats, heel raises, resistance band exercises, ankle dorsiflexion strengthening
Balance training: static and dynamic exercises on firm and foam surfaces, tandem stance practice, single-leg stance progression, perturbation training
Fall prevention education and environmental modification
Home exercise program instruction and progression

sixMinuteWalkTest.interpretation: Clinically meaningful improvement (MCID for HF patients: 30-36 meters). Still below predicted norms but significant functional gain. SpO2 nadir improved from 91% to 93% during test.

timedUpAndGo.interpretation: Improved from high fall risk to borderline fall risk category. Patient now rises from chair without armrest assistance.

bergBalanceScale.interpretation: Moved from moderate to low-moderate fall risk. Improved in tandem stance (3→5), reaching forward (2→3), and turning 360 degrees (2→3).

gripStrength.interpretation: Modest improvement, still below age norms

functionalStatus.ambulation: Independent in home without assistive device on level surfaces. Uses single handrail for stairs (3 steps to front porch) — step-over-step pattern ascending, step-to pattern descending. Community ambulation limited by exertional dyspnea — can walk approximately 1 block (150-200 feet) on level ground before needing to rest.
functionalStatus.transfers: Independent sit-to-stand from standard height chair and bed. Independent toilet and tub transfers with grab bars.
functionalStatus.adls: Independent with all basic ADLs including bathing (uses shower chair), dressing, grooming, toileting
functionalStatus.iadls: Independent with light meal preparation, medication management. Requires assistance with heavy housework, laundry (carrying basket), grocery shopping. Wife continues to drive for all errands.
functionalStatus.exerciseProgram: Patient demonstrated independence with full home exercise program. Performs daily: 15-minute walk (AM), seated leg exercises, standing balance exercises with countertop for safety. Adherence has been excellent — wife provides encouragement and occasionally exercises alongside him.

sections.gaitAtDischarge: Improved gait pattern compared to initial evaluation. Base of support narrowed. Stride length improved. Bilateral foot drop persists (neuropathy — not expected to improve with PT) but patient demonstrates improved compensatory strategies with hip flexion clearance. Cadence improved. No longer 'furniture walks.'

shortTermGoals.goal: Safe household ambulation without UE support

longTermGoals.status: Met — area rugs removed, shower chair in use, nightlights installed
Partially met — can walk to mailbox and approximately 1 block; church parking lot achievable with rest break

longTermGoals.goal: Independent stair navigation with single handrail
longTermGoals.status: Met ascending, partially met descending (uses step-to pattern)

sections.assessment:
Mr. Morrison has made meaningful functional gains over 6 weeks of home health PT. He has exceeded most short-term goals and met or partially met long-term goals. His 6MWT improved by 50 meters (28%), TUG improved by 4 seconds (22%), and Berg Balance Scale improved by 6 points. He is now independent with household ambulation, transfers, and ADLs. Community ambulation remains limited by exertional dyspnea but has improved from baseline.

Persistent limitations include: bilateral foot drop (neuropathic, will not resolve with PT), exertional dyspnea limiting community distance, and ongoing fall risk (though significantly reduced). Further gains would likely require a supervised cardiac rehabilitation program where exercise intensity can be more precisely monitored and advanced.

Patient has reached a plateau for home-based PT services and is appropriate for discharge.

items: Continue daily walking program: 15 minutes AM walk, can gradually advance to 20 minutes if tolerated
Continue seated and standing strengthening exercises 3x/week (program provided in writing)
Continue balance exercises daily — tandem stance, single-leg stance with countertop support
Monitor symptoms during exercise: stop if excessive dyspnea, chest pain, or lightheadedness
Strongly recommend outpatient cardiac rehabilitation program — patient qualifies with HFrEF diagnosis
Would benefit from supervised, progressive exercise with telemetry monitoring
Discussed with patient — he is willing but concerned about transportation (30-minute drive to nearest cardiac rehab facility). Wife willing to drive but schedule may be challenging.
Referral placed to Regional Medical Center Cardiac Rehab program
Continue environmental modifications: no throw rugs, adequate lighting, shower chair use
Wear sturdy, well-fitting shoes at all times (no slippers, no bare feet)
Consider ankle-foot orthoses (AFOs) if foot drop worsens — discuss with neurology or physiatry
If functional decline noted, re-refer to PT
Patient has not yet started cardiac rehab — recommend follow-up on this referral
Exertional oxygen desaturation noted (SpO2 nadir 91-93%) — may benefit from formal cardiopulmonary exercise testing or ambulatory O2 assessment
Foot drop bilateral — may warrant neurology reassessment if progressive

sections.plan:
1. Discharge from home health PT services — goals met, patient at plateau for home-based therapy
2. Patient independent with home exercise program
3. Cardiac rehab referral placed — recommend follow-up on enrollment
4. Re-refer to PT if functional decline or new fall event
5. Written HEP provided to patient and wife

attestation:
I personally evaluated this patient, performed all discharge assessments, and formulated the discharge plan. Home exercise program reviewed and demonstrated with patient and wife.

Electronically signed by: Sarah Thompson, PT, DPT
Date/Time: 10/6/2022 1:00 PM
